title: JSON Formatter & Validator
metaDescription: Free online JSON formatter and validator. Paste raw JSON to beautify, validate, and detect syntax errors instantly in your browser — no data leaves your device.
shortDescription: Paste raw JSON to format, validate, and catch syntax errors instantly.

guideSections.heading: Why Client-Side Validation Matters for Sensitive Payloads
guideSections.body: Pasting API responses or configuration files containing tokens or internal identifiers into a server-side formatting tool means that data leaves your machine and passes through someone else's infrastructure. This formatter runs entirely in your browser using the native JSON.parse() method — nothing you paste is ever transmitted anywhere, which matters when the JSON you're debugging contains anything you wouldn't want logged on a third-party server.

guideSections.heading: Why Syntax Errors in JSON Are Often Misleading
guideSections.body: JSON parsers report the position of the character where parsing failed, not necessarily where the actual mistake is. A missing closing brace three lines up commonly surfaces as an error several lines later, at the point where the parser's expectations finally break down. Reading the reported line number as a strong hint rather than a precise location saves time when debugging deeply nested structures.

guideSections.heading: Trailing Commas Are the Most Common Hand-Written JSON Mistake
guideSections.body: Unlike JavaScript object literals, strict JSON does not permit a trailing comma after the last item in an array or object. Code copied from a JS file into a JSON config file frequently carries this over invisibly, since most editors don't flag it until the file is actually parsed as JSON rather than as JavaScript.

guideSections.heading: When to Validate vs When to Just Format
guideSections.body: Formatting (indentation, line breaks) and validation (syntax correctness) are different operations that often get bundled together. A file can be syntactically valid JSON and still be poorly formatted for human review, or appear readable while containing a subtle error a formatter alone won't catch. Running both steps separately, rather than assuming one implies the other, catches more real-world issues.
